questions.question:
A trapezium has vertices marked as P, Q, R and S (in that order anticlockwise). The side PQ is parallel to side SR. Further, it is given that PQ = 11 cm, QR = 4 cm, RS = 6 cm and SP = 3 cm. What is the shortest distance between PQ and SR (in cm)?

A. 1.80   B. 2.40   C. 4.20   D. 5.76
questions.to_find: shortest distance (perpendicular height) between the parallel sides PQ and SR of the trapezium

understand.plain: We have a trapezium PQRS with PQ parallel to SR. The shortest distance between two parallel lines is the perpendicular distance between them, which is the height of the trapezium. By placing PQ along the x-axis and letting the height be h, we can set up equations using the known leg lengths SP = 3 and QR = 4 to solve for h.

keywords.explain: A quadrilateral with exactly one pair of parallel sides, here PQ || SR.
keywords.example: A traffic sign shaped like a trapezoid: one side is 11 cm, the opposite shorter side is 6 cm.

keywords.explain: The perpendicular distance between the two parallel sides; this is the height h of the trapezium.
keywords.example: For a trapezium with bases on y=0 and y=h, the shortest distance is exactly h.

keywords.explain: Place one base on the x-axis and use distance formulas with the non-parallel sides to form equations in the unknown height.
keywords.example: P at origin, Q at (11,0), S at (a, h), R at (a+6, h); then SP^2 = a^2 + h^2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 360 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='360' height='180' class='bg' rx='8'/><polygon points='30,150 195,150 183,50 66,50' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='66' y1='50' x2='66' y2='150' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='66' y1='148' x2='74' y2='148' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='74' y1='148' x2='74' y2='140' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1'/><text x='26' y='167' class='lbl ink'>P</text><text x='191' y='167' class='lbl ink'>Q</text><text x='185' y='44' class='lbl ink'>R</text><text x='55' y='44' class='lbl ink'>S</text><text x='108' y='170' class='sub mut'>PQ = 11 cm</text><text x='108' y='40' class='sub mut'>SR = 6 cm</text><text x='196' y='108' class='sub mut'>QR = 4</text><text x='18' y='108' class='sub mut'>SP = 3</text><text x='77' y='108' class='tag ok'>h = ?</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Trapezium PQRS with PQ=11 on the base, SR=6 on top, legs SP=3 and QR=4, and a dashed vertical height line h.

given.aim: find the perpendicular height h between the parallel sides PQ and SR

terms.meaning: The base of the trapezium placed along the x-axis from P=(0,0) to Q=(11,0).
terms.example: Sets the coordinate frame for the entire problem.
terms.connects: Fixes the x-positions of P and Q so we can express S and R in terms of unknown a and h.

terms.meaning: The top side of the trapezium; since PQ || SR, both S and R share the same y-coordinate h.
terms.example: S=(a, h) and R=(a+6, h) automatically satisfy SR = 6.
terms.connects: Relates the x-offset a of S to the x-position of R via R = a + 6.

terms.term: SP = 3 cm and QR = 4 cm (non-parallel legs)
terms.meaning: The two slanting sides give two distance equations involving the unknowns a and h.
terms.connects: Subtracting these equations eliminates h^2 and yields a linear equation in a, which then gives h.

given.plan: Place PQ on the x-axis. Let S = (a, h) and R = (a+6, h). Write SP^2 = 9 and QR^2 = 16. Subtract to find a = 1.8, then compute h = sqrt(9 - a^2) = sqrt(9 - 3.24) = sqrt(5.76) = 2.4 cm.

steps.title: Set up coordinates
steps.apply: Place P at the origin and Q at (11,0). Since PQ || SR, points S and R lie at height h. With SR=6, we have R at x-coordinate a+6 if S is at x-coordinate a.
steps.note: The parameter a is the horizontal offset of S from P; it can be positive or negative.

steps.title: Write distance equations for the legs
steps.apply: SP = 3 gives a^2 + h^2 = 9. QR = 4 gives (a-5)^2 + h^2 = 16. Expanding: a^2 - 10a + 25 + h^2 = 16.
steps.note: Both equations share the h^2 term, which cancels when we subtract.

steps.title: Solve for a by subtracting the two equations
steps.note: h^2 cancels cleanly, leaving a pure linear equation in a.

solution.result: The shortest distance between PQ and SR is 2.40 cm.

questions.formula_note: No standard registry formula applies; the solution uses the Euclidean distance formula and algebraic elimination — both elementary tools.

questions.question:
Four identical cylindrical chalk-sticks, each of radius r = 0.5 cm and length l = 10 cm, are bound tightly together using a duct tape as shown in the following figure. The width of the duct tape is equal to the length of the chalk-stick. The area (in cm^2) of the duct tape required to wrap the bundle of chalk-sticks once is:

A. 2(4 + pi)   B. 20(4 + pi)   C. 10(8 + pi)   D. 10(4 + pi)
questions.to_find: area of duct tape required to wrap the bundle of four chalk-sticks once

understand.plain: Four identical cylinders of radius 0.5 cm are arranged in a 2x2 square packing so that each touches its two neighbours. The duct tape wraps around the outside of this bundle in one loop. Since the tape width equals the chalk-stick length (10 cm), the area of tape used equals the perimeter of the cross-section of the bundle multiplied by 10 cm. The cross-section perimeter consists of four straight tangent segments and four quarter-circle arcs.

keywords.explain: Four cylinders arranged so their centres form a square; each cylinder touches two others. The centre-to-centre distance between adjacent cylinders is 2r.
keywords.example: Imagine four coins lying flat in a 2x2 grid, each touching its two neighbours.

keywords.explain: The outer boundary seen when looking at the bundle end-on. It has four straight sections (one per flat side, length 2r each) and four quarter-circle corner arcs (radius r each, total arc = one full circle = 2*pi*r).
keywords.example: Like a rounded square (stadium shape extended to a square): 4 flat sides + 4 curved corners.

keywords.explain: When a rectangular strip of tape wraps once around a surface, the area of tape used equals the circumference (perimeter of cross-section) times the width of the tape.
keywords.example: A strip of tape 7 cm long and 10 cm wide covers 70 cm^2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 260 260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='260' height='260' class='bg' rx='8'/><circle cx='90' cy='90' r='40' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='170' cy='90' r='40' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='90' cy='170' r='40' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='170' cy='170' r='40' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M90,50 L170,50 A40,40 0 0,1 210,90 L210,170 A40,40 0,0,1 170,210 L90,210 A40,40 0,0,1 50,170 L50,90 A40,40 0,0,1 90,50 Z' fill='none' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><text x='118' y='134' class='sub mut'>2r</text><line x1='90' y1='130' x2='170' y2='130' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='85' y1='126' x2='85' y2='134' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='175' y1='126' x2='175' y2='134' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1'/><text x='50' y='130' class='sub ok'>tape</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: End-on view of four cylinders in a 2x2 arrangement; the green dashed outline shows the tape path: four straight sections and four quarter-circle corners.

given.aim: calculate the area of duct tape that wraps once around the 4-cylinder bundle

terms.meaning: The radius determines both the straight-section lengths (2r each) and the arc radii (r each) of the cross-section perimeter.
terms.example: Adjacent cylinder centres are 2r = 1 cm apart; the straight tangent segment between them is also 2r = 1 cm long.
terms.connects: Feeds directly into the perimeter calculation: 4*(2r) + 2*pi*r.

terms.term: l = 10 cm (length of each chalk-stick and width of tape)
terms.meaning: Because the tape width equals the stick length, the tape covers the full length of the bundle in one wrap.
terms.connects: Multiplies the perimeter to give the total area of tape used.

terms.meaning: Consists of 4 straight tangent sections (each of length 2r = 1 cm) and 4 quarter-circle arcs (each of radius r = 0.5 cm), totalling 4+pi cm.
terms.example: Straight total: 4*(1) = 4 cm. Arc total: 4*(pi/2)*(0.5) = pi cm. Perimeter = (4+pi) cm.
terms.connects: Is multiplied by l=10 to give the tape area 10(4+pi) cm^2.

given.plan: Find the perimeter of the outer cross-section: 4 straight sections each of length 2r, plus 4 quarter-circle arcs of radius r (totalling one full circle). Multiply this perimeter by the tape width l = 10 cm to get the tape area.

steps.title: Identify the cross-section path of the tape
steps.apply: In the 2x2 arrangement each pair of adjacent cylinders shares a common external tangent of length equal to the centre-to-centre spacing minus nothing (they touch), so the straight section between each pair of adjacent cylinder centres is 2r = 1 cm. At each of the four outer corners the tape follows a quarter-circle of radius r = 0.5 cm.
steps.note: The four quarter-circles together form one complete circle of radius r.

steps.title: Compute total straight length and arc length
steps.apply: L_straight = 4 * 2*(0.5) = 4 cm. L_arc = 2*pi*(0.5) = pi cm. Total perimeter = 4 + pi cm.

steps.title: Multiply perimeter by tape width to get area
steps.apply: The tape wraps once around the bundle; its area = length of tape * width of tape = (4+pi) * 10 = 10(4+pi) cm^2.

solution.result: Area of duct tape = 10(4 + pi) cm^2.

questions.formula_note: The problem uses basic arc-length and area-of-rectangle formulas; no registry entry was required.

questions.question:
A rhombus is formed by joining the midpoints of the sides of a unit square. What is the diameter of the largest circle that can be inscribed within the rhombus?

A. 1/sqrt(2)   B. 1/sqrt(2)   C. sqrt(2)   D. 2*sqrt(2)
questions.to_find: diameter of the largest circle that can be inscribed in the rhombus formed by joining midpoints of a unit square

understand.plain: When we join the four midpoints of a unit square's sides in order, we get a smaller rhombus (actually a square) tilted 45 degrees. This rhombus has diagonals of length 1 each and side length 1/sqrt(2). The inscribed circle (incircle) touches all four sides; its radius equals the area of the rhombus divided by its semi-perimeter. The diameter is twice that radius.

keywords.term: rhombus from midpoints of a square
keywords.explain: Joining the four midpoints of a unit square creates a quadrilateral that is itself a square (special case of rhombus) with side 1/sqrt(2) and diagonals each of length 1.
keywords.example: Unit square vertices at (0,0),(1,0),(1,1),(0,1); midpoints at (0.5,0),(1,0.5),(0.5,1),(0,0.5). The resulting shape has equal sides and equal diagonals so it is a square-rhombus.

keywords.explain: Every rhombus has an inscribed circle that touches all four sides. Its radius r = Area / semi-perimeter.
keywords.example: For a rhombus with area A and perimeter P, the inradius is r = 2A/P.

keywords.explain: The diameter of the inscribed circle is twice the inradius.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 260 260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='260' height='260' class='bg' rx='8'/><rect x='30' y='30' width='200' height='200' fill='none' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><polygon points='130,30 230,130 130,230 30,130' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='130' cy='130' r='70.7' fill='none' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='130' y1='30' x2='130' y2='230' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3,3'/><line x1='30' y1='130' x2='230' y2='130' stroke='var(--mut,#5F5E5A)' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3,3'/><text x='98' y='18' class='sub ink'>(0.5, 0)</text><text x='232' y='134' class='sub ink'>(1, 0.5)</text><text x='88' y='248' class='sub ink'>(0.5, 1)</text><text x='4' y='134' class='sub ink'>(0, 0.5)</text><text x='96' y='128' class='tag ok'>r</text><line x1='130' y1='130' x2='130' y2='200.7' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='133' y='170' class='sub ok'>r</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Unit square (dashed) with the inner rhombus formed by its midpoints shown in purple, and the inscribed circle shown in green touching all four sides.

given.aim: find the diameter of the largest circle fitting inside the rhombus

terms.meaning: The outer square from which the rhombus is derived; vertices at (0,0),(1,0),(1,1),(0,1).
terms.example: Starting shape with all sides equal to 1 cm.
terms.connects: Defines the midpoints that become the vertices of the inner rhombus.

terms.meaning: The two diagonals of the inner rhombus both have length 1 (spanning the full width/height of the unit square).
terms.example: Diagonal from (0.5,0) to (0.5,1) has length 1; diagonal from (0,0.5) to (1,0.5) also has length 1.
terms.connects: Used to compute both the area and the side length of the rhombus.

terms.meaning: Each side of the inner rhombus equals the distance between adjacent midpoints, which is sqrt((0.5)^2+(0.5)^2) = 1/sqrt(2).
terms.connects: Used in the semi-perimeter to find the inradius.

terms.meaning: For any polygon with an inscribed circle, the inradius equals the area divided by the semi-perimeter.

given.plan: Find the midpoints of the unit square's sides, compute the side length and area of the resulting rhombus, then apply the incircle formula r = Area/semi-perimeter. The diameter = 2r.

steps.title: Identify the rhombus vertices
steps.apply: The midpoints of the four sides of the unit square are M1=(0.5,0), M2=(1,0.5), M3=(0.5,1), M4=(0,0.5). Joining these in order gives a rhombus.
steps.note: Because the original square is regular, the resulting shape is actually a square (all sides and all angles equal), which is a special rhombus.

steps.title: Compute the side length and diagonals
steps.apply: Side: distance from M1 to M2 = sqrt((1-0.5)^2+(0.5-0)^2) = sqrt(0.5) = 1/sqrt(2). Diagonals: M1 to M3 = 1, M2 to M4 = 1.
steps.note: Equal diagonals confirm the rhombus is a square rotated 45 degrees.

steps.title: Compute the area of the rhombus
steps.apply: Using the diagonal formula for the area of a rhombus: Area = (d1*d2)/2 = (1*1)/2 = 0.5 sq cm.
steps.note: This is exactly half the area of the unit square, as expected from the midpoint construction.

steps.title: Apply the inradius formula and compute diameter

solution.result: The diameter of the largest inscribed circle = 1/sqrt(2) cm.

questions.formula_note: The diagonal area formula for a rhombus (Area = d1*d2/2) and the incircle radius formula (r = Area/semi-perimeter) are both classical results not requiring a registry entry.
